preamble: The phonetic transformation from the voiced palatal approximant /j/ to the voiceless glottal fricative /h/ is a documented sound change, primarily occurring through a process known as debuccalization. This involves the weakening of an oral consonant, where its place of articulation shifts to the glottis, often accompanied by a loss of voicing and a change in manner from approximant to fricative.

examples.note: In the standard variety of Yoruba, the glide /j/ undergoes debuccalization to the glottal fricative /h/ before nasalized homorganic vowels, often in intervocalic contexts.
In Proto-Greek, initial *j was replaced by /z/ (possibly [dz]) or was lost.
The /j/ sound in borrowings like 'Yovsep' (from Hebrew Yosef) became /h/ in Armenian.

sources: Bate, D. L. (2020). The Un-mouthing of Sounds.
O'Brien, J. (2010). Debuccalization and supplementary gestures.
Cambridge University Press. (2014). The Evolution of Ancient Maya Exchange Systems: An Etymological Study of Economic Vocabulary in the Mayan Language Family.
